name: Self-Determination Theory applied to media (Tamborini, Ryan, Rigby)
core_claim: Media enjoyment is fundamentally the satisfaction of three innate, universal psychological needs: autonomy (a sense of volition and control over one's choices), competence (feeling capable and effective), and relatedness (feeling connected to others). Tamborini, Bowman, Eden, Grizzard & Organ (2010) reframe 'enjoyment' itself — long treated as a fuzzy outcome variable in communication research — as the experiential by-product of intrinsic need satisfaction during media use. Their experiment manipulating video-game features tied to each need explained ~51% of the variance in enjoyment WITHOUT invoking simple hedonic pleasure-seeking, implying that need satisfaction, not pleasure per se, is the deeper driver. Ryan, Rigby & Przybylski (2006) independently developed the parallel Player Experience of Need Satisfaction (PENS) model, arguing the 'motivational pull' of games is the degree to which play affords these three need satisfactions, and that all three contribute independently to enjoyment, immersion and intention to keep playing. The central claim for a recommender is therefore: what makes a work satisfying is not primarily its topic or even its felt affect, but the way it gives the receiver a sense of agency, mastery, and connection.
descriptive_vs_experiential: SDT operates at a MOTIVATIONAL/EXPERIENTIAL level distinct from both the descriptive ('what it is about') and the purely affective ('what it feels like, valence/arousal') dimensions. It is not topical — autonomy/competence/relatedness are not subjects a story is 'about'. It is also not simply affective in the mood-state sense; it explains WHY a media experience is rewarding by reference to underlying need fulfilment, which is a motivational construct. It is closest to the experiential axis but adds a causal/functional layer: it specifies the psychological mechanism that produces enjoyment. Crucially, SDT in its origins (and in PENS) is most natural for INTERACTIVE media (games), where the viewer literally exercises agency. Applied to non-interactive TV/film/anime it becomes more vicarious: autonomy is experienced through identification with characters who exercise volition, competence through mastery shown on screen or through narrative comprehension the viewer achieves, relatedness through parasocial bonds and felt connection to characters and to co-viewers. So for HelpME2C's medium it occupies an experiential-motivational level that is partly a property of the work (the affordances it offers) and partly a property of the receiver (whether they take up those affordances).
key_dimensions: Three core need-satisfaction axes (the candidate vocabulary additions): (1) AUTONOMY — the work affords a felt sense of volition, meaningful choice, self-direction, freedom; vicariously, protagonists who exercise agency, defy constraint, choose their path; meta-level: nonlinear/branching narratives, anthology freedom. (2) COMPETENCE — the work affords mastery, effectance, the satisfaction of solving/understanding/succeeding; vicariously, characters demonstrating skill and earned victory (underdog-rise, heist mastery), or the viewer's own pleasure of solving (mystery/whodunit puzzle-solving). (3) RELATEDNESS — the work affords felt connection, belonging, intimacy, mattering to others; vicariously, found-family, deep friendships, romance, ensemble warmth; socially, co-viewing and shared-experience bonding. Extensions add a fourth cluster from the 2011 Tamborini et al. follow-up ('Media Enjoyment as Need Satisfaction: The Contribution of Hedonic and Nonhedonic Needs'): NONHEDONIC / EUDAIMONIC need satisfaction — needs for meaningfulness, insight, and value affirmation alongside the three intrinsic needs and simple hedonic pleasure. PENS adds two game-specific facets useful as analogues: PRESENCE/IMMERSION and INTUITIVE CONTROLS (less transferable to non-interactive media).
cross_cultural_validity: SDT explicitly claims the three needs are innate and UNIVERSAL — the strongest cross-cultural claim of any framework in this set. This claim is the most contested part of the theory. The standing critique (rooted in Markus & Kitayama-style cultural psychology) is that autonomy is an individualistic, Western value that does not translate to collectivist societies; some studies find autonomy a weak predictor of motivation/performance in East Asian samples (e.g. work cited on Chinese students), and that externally-directed action can sustain intrinsic motivation in collectivist contexts. SDT proponents counter that the critique conflates AUTONOMY (volition/self-endorsement of action) with INDEPENDENCE (acting alone): one can autonomously endorse a collective or authority-directed goal. Recent confirmatory work (Nalipay, King & Cai, 2020, 'Autonomy is equally important across East and West') reports that need satisfaction predicts well-being even controlling for collectivism, supporting universality. Net assessment for the taxonomy: the three needs are well-validated cross-culturally at the level of WELL-BEING outcomes, but the EXPRESSION and salience of each need (especially autonomy) varies by culture, and the bulk of media-specific validation (games, US/Western/German samples) is Western-centric. The relatedness and competence axes travel better cross-culturally than autonomy. This matters for anime (Japanese collectivist-leaning production context) vs Western TV: a work satisfying relatedness via group harmony may read as autonomy-suppressing to a Western coder but as need-satisfying to its source audience.
empirical_grounding: Among the stronger empirically grounded frameworks here. Evidence is EXPERIMENTAL at its core: Tamborini et al. (2010) manipulated game features tied to each need in a controlled experiment and validated a structural model explaining ~51% of enjoyment variance. Ryan, Rigby & Przybylski (2006) ran multiple studies (survey + experimental) validating PENS, showing each need independently predicts enjoyment, immersion and future-play intention. The 2011 follow-up extended the model with hedonic/nonhedonic needs, also experimentally. Beyond games there is a large correlational literature applying SDT to media (including streaming/VOD gratifications). Quality: high internal validity for games; weaker for non-interactive TV/film/anime, where most evidence is correlational and survey-based rather than experimental, and where the 'agency' construct is vicarious rather than literal. To validate LLM-extracted SDT dimensions you would need: (a) a labelled corpus where works are scored for the need-affordances they offer, ideally by trained human coders, and (b) viewer-side need-satisfaction self-reports (e.g. PENS or BPNS scales) linked to actual enjoyment/completion data, so that work-level affordance labels can be correlated against realised satisfaction. Without the receiver-side data, you can validate extraction reliability (inter-coder agreement vs LLM) but not predictive validity for satisfaction. [uncertain] The exact predictive lift of SDT need-affordance labels over genre/topic baselines for NON-interactive recommendation is not established in the literature I surveyed.
operationalisability: PARTIALLY extractable from a synopsis, with significant caveats. RELATEDNESS is the most extractable: synopses readily signal found-family, friendship, romance, ensemble bonds, loneliness (its frustration). COMPETENCE is moderately extractable via proxies: underdog-rise, heist/caper mastery, mystery-solving, mentorship, skill-and-training arcs. AUTONOMY is the hardest and most error-prone: a synopsis rarely states whether a protagonist acts volitionally vs is swept along; the coder must infer agency from plot, which an LLM will frequently over- or under-read. Extraction unit: best at WHOLE-WORK or ARC level for the affordance/theme signal; scene level is too granular for synopsis data. Failure modes: (1) the core SDT construct is the RECEIVER'S need satisfaction, which a synopsis cannot contain — the LLM can at best label need-RELEVANT CONTENT/AFFORDANCES, not satisfaction, and risks presenting an affordance label as if it were an experiential guarantee. (2) Autonomy/independence conflation — LLM may tag any 'lone protagonist defies authority' plot as autonomy when the construct is volition, not solitude. (3) Vicarious vs literal: SDT's strongest evidence is interactive; an LLM trained on game-flavoured definitions may mis-map to passive viewing. (4) Cultural mis-coding of autonomy in non-Western works (see cross-cultural field). Recommendation: extract need-AFFORDANCE tags (does this work foreground agency / mastery / connection?) as work-level signals, and treat actual need satisfaction as a downstream, receiver-dependent inference — never claim it from synopsis alone.
motivation_served: SDT spans and partly subsumes both hedonic and eudaimonic motivations, but reframes them: simple hedonic pleasure (fun, escapism) is treated as ONE input, while the deeper rewards are need satisfactions that can be either hedonic (competence/relatedness feeling good) or eudaimonic (autonomy + meaning/insight from the 2011 nonhedonic extension, mapping to 'being moved' and value affirmation). So it maps cleanly onto the hedonic/eudaimonic split other frameworks use, but adds a mechanism. For GROUP viewing this is the framework's most interesting property: two people may seek satisfaction of DIFFERENT needs from the same session — one wants relatedness (a warm ensemble to feel connected through), another wants competence (a tight puzzle to solve). A good joint recommendation either (a) finds a work high on the needs BOTH share, or (b) finds a work that affords distinct needs simultaneously (e.g. a mystery with found-family — competence for one, relatedness for the other). Relatedness is also uniquely SOCIAL: the act of co-viewing itself satisfies relatedness, so the framework supports the North Star directly — watching together is itself need-satisfying, independent of content.
receiver_dependence: HIGHLY receiver-dependent — this is the framework's defining feature and its key risk for a recommender. The actual constructs (need SATISFACTION) are emergent interactions between the work's affordances and a specific viewer's traits, mood, current need-deprivation, and skill. The SAME work satisfies competence strongly for a viewer who engages the puzzle and weakly for one who passively watches. Autonomy satisfaction depends on whether the viewer values/takes up the agency offered. What CAN be treated as a (relatively) work-level property is the AFFORDANCE — the opportunity the work offers for each need — but even affordances are filtered through receiver disposition more than, say, gore level (which is absolute). Practical encoding: SDT need-affordance tags belong on the RELATIONAL side of HelpME2C's model, not the absolute side. For two-person matching, autonomy and competence affordances should be weighted by each user's revealed preference (do they historically enjoy agency-/mastery-heavy works?), while relatedness has a partially absolute component (the co-viewing act) plus a relational one (do these two people bond over warm-ensemble content?).
blind_spots: (1) PLEASURE AND THRILL UNDER-WEIGHTED: by design SDT downplays simple hedonic arousal — pure spectacle, visceral horror thrill, comedic delight, sensory beauty — yet these are huge drivers of TV/anime appeal and are not well captured by autonomy/competence/relatedness. (2) NEGATIVE/PAINFUL ENJOYMENT (the 'tragedy paradox'): SDT struggles to explain why people seek out grief, dread, or distress (existential horror, war-and-its-toll); the 2011 nonhedonic extension patches this with meaning-needs but it remains a bolt-on. (3) AESTHETIC/CRAFT APPRECIATION: enjoying cinematography, prose, score, or formal innovation (nonlinear-storytelling, meditation-on-time) is not a need satisfaction in the SDT sense. (4) INTERACTIVITY BIAS: the theory is strongest where the viewer acts; for passive viewing the autonomy construct becomes strained and vicarious, the weakest leg. (5) NOVELTY/CURIOSITY: arguably a distinct drive (first-contact, mystery's epistemic pull) only partly reducible to competence. (6) It says little about CONTENT-LEVEL deal-breakers (violence, sexual content) that matter enormously for group co-viewing acceptability. (7) It cannot, from work data alone, tell you WHICH need a given viewer is currently deprived of and seeking — the most predictive variable is invisible to it.
vocabulary_implications: Recommendation: do NOT add autonomy/competence/relatedness as literal theme slugs (they are motivational constructs, not themes, and over-reach what a synopsis can support). Instead, treat them as a THREE-AXIS MOTIVATIONAL OVERLAY computed FROM existing theme slugs, plus add a small number of affordance tags where existing vocabulary has gaps. Proposed encoding: (A) Map existing slugs to need-affordances as a derived layer — RELATEDNESS-affording: found-family, friendship-tested, platonic-soulmates, ensemble-mosaic, forbidden-love, mentor-and-pupil (and relatedness-FRUSTRATION: loneliness-and-isolation, parent-child-rift, betrayal). COMPETENCE-affording: underdog-rise, heist-and-caper, mystery-investigation, whodunit, workplace-procedural, mentor-and-pupil, survival-and-endurance. AUTONOMY-affording: revolution-and-rebellion, finding-purpose, coming-of-age, fish-out-of-water, vigilante-justice (autonomy-FRUSTRATION: civilian-under-occupation, dystopian-society, institutional-failure). (B) New slugs to add for genuine gaps: 'mastery-and-skill' (the training/getting-good arc — competence affordance not covered by underdog-rise which is about status), 'agency-reclaimed' (protagonist seizing volition from constraint — a cleaner autonomy signal than the politically-loaded revolution slugs), and optionally 'shared-experience' / co-viewing-friendly flag (relatedness via the act of watching together — directly North-Star relevant). (C) NOT extractable from synopsis and requiring different data: actual need satisfaction (needs viewer self-report / PENS-style scales), per-user need-deprivation state (needs behavioural/mood data), and the autonomy distinction from independence (needs careful coder guidelines, high LLM error). Flag the three need axes as RELATIONAL/receiver-weighted scores in the matching model, not absolute content tags.
relationship_to_existing_vocabulary: VALIDATES a large cluster of relationship/emotion slugs as the relatedness backbone: found-family, friendship-tested, platonic-soulmates, ensemble-mosaic, forbidden-love, unrequited-longing, and their frustration counterparts loneliness-and-isolation, parent-child-rift, toxic-relationship. These are arguably the most predictive slugs SDT endorses, and it suggests relatedness-affordance is currently the best-served need in the vocabulary. VALIDATES competence-affording slugs: underdog-rise, heist-and-caper, mystery-investigation, whodunit, workplace-procedural, survival-and-endurance, mentor-and-pupil. REVEALS GAPS on the autonomy axis — the vocabulary has no clean autonomy slug; the nearest (revolution-and-rebellion, vigilante-justice, finding-purpose) are topically loaded and conflate autonomy with politics or solitude, so SDT suggests adding 'agency-reclaimed' and 'mastery-and-skill'. SUGGESTS MISALIGNMENT: nothing in the vocabulary captures the co-viewing/social-relatedness affordance that is most directly tied to the North Star — a 'shared-experience' flag is missing. Texture slugs (slow-burn, character-study, meditation-on-time/death) are largely INVISIBLE to SDT — it offers no purchase on aesthetic/craft appreciation, confirming those slugs serve a motivation SDT does not model. Genre/structure slugs like nonlinear-storytelling and anthology relate weakly to autonomy at the meta level but SDT does not strongly motivate them. Net: SDT endorses the relationship and competence clusters, flags two autonomy gaps and one social-relatedness gap, and stays silent on the texture cluster.
key_sources: Tamborini, Bowman, Eden, Grizzard & Organ (2010) 'Defining Media Enjoyment as the Satisfaction of Intrinsic Needs,' Journal of Communication 60(4):758-777, doi:10.1111/j.1460-2466.2010.01513.x — FREE author PDF: https://selfdeterminationtheory.org/SDT/documents/2010_TamboriniBowmanetal_JC.pdf (publisher paywalled: https://academic.oup.com/joc/article-abstract/60/4/758/4098566). Tamborini, Grizzard, Bowman, Reinecke, Lewis & Eden (2011) 'Media Enjoyment as Need Satisfaction: The Contribution of Hedonic and Nonhedonic Needs,' Journal of Communication 61(6) (hedonic/eudaimonic extension): https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1111/j.1460-2466.2011.01593.x (paywalled; ResearchGate copy: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/241852437). Ryan, Rigby & Przybylski (2006) 'The Motivational Pull of Video Games: A Self-Determination Theory Approach,' Motivation and Emotion — PENS origin: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/225998888 ; PENS overview FREE: https://selfdeterminationtheory.org/player-experience-of-needs-satisfaction-pens/. Przybylski, Rigby & Ryan (2010) 'A Motivational Model of Video Game Engagement,' Review of General Psychology — FREE PDF: https://selfdeterminationtheory.org/SDT/documents/2010_PrzybylskiRigbyRyan_ROGP.pdf. Cross-cultural: Nalipay, King & Cai (2020) 'Autonomy is equally important across East and West,' Journal of Adolescence — FREE PDF: https://selfdeterminationtheory.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/02/2020_NalipayaKingbCai_UniversalityofSDT.pdf. Foundational: Deci & Ryan, Self-Determination Theory (theory hub, free): https://selfdeterminationtheory.org/theory/.
